notes: Embedded-MBID release matches exactly: title, artist Al Di Meola, 1996-11-05, Mesa/Bluemoon Recordings. All 5 keyTracks present in the MB tracklist. The albumDNA personnel/composition claims (8 of 10 songs by Piazzolla; percussionist Arto Tuncboyacian; Dino Saluzzi on bandoneon; Vince Mendoza string arrangements) cannot be checked because MB ships no personnel/credits — none are contradicted. Entity is clearly correct; flagged unverifiable only for the uncheckable personnel claims.

Embedded MBID and stored wiki URL both resolve to this album. albumDNA is taken near-verbatim from the Wikipedia extract and every claim (collaborative album by Holdsworth/Jens Johansson/Anders Johansson; 1996 Heptagon Records Sweden; 12 Aug 1997 Shrapnel + Pony Canyon Japan) is confirmed. All 5 keyTracks in the tracklist. Crediting Allan Holdsworth alone as the artist is legitimate editorial choice (MB lists all three).

Embedded-MBID release matches title, date 1996, tracklist exactly. Tina Brooks is among the credited MB artists (Howard McGhee, Tina Brooks, Milt Hinton, Osie Johnson), so crediting her is fine. Both tracks named in the prose and all 5 keyTracks are on the release. Label 'Unknown' (MB ships no label) is honest, not a contradiction. Enough is checkable to confirm the right entity.

MB search match (sim 1.0) and stored wiki URL both about Cassandra Wilson's New Moon Daughter. All 4 keyTracks (Strange Fruit, Last Train to Clarksville, Harvest Moon, Death Letter) are on the MB tracklist. The Billboard No.1 and Grammy (Best Jazz Vocal Performance) claims are taken verbatim from the entity-matched Wikipedia page. Year discrepancy to note, not flag: prose says 'released by Blue Note in 1995' (matches the wiki extract and wiki short-description, both '1995') while the year field and MB release date are 1996 (likely recording vs release). Prose is faithful to its entity-matched wiki source.

Embedded-MBID release confirms artist Allan Holdsworth, 1996-03-15, Verve Forecast (JP). All 5 keyTracks present in the tracklist. The detailed release-history claims (ninth studio album; 24 Sep 1996 via Polydor Japan / JMS-Cream Europe / Restless Records; remastered 17 Apr 2012 MoonJune Records) are not in the single MB release shown and cannot be verified — but they are not contradicted by it. Unverifiable on those discography/release claims.

Embedded MBID and stored wiki URL both about this album. '1996 album by Oscar Peterson, nominated for a 1997 Juno Award for Best Mainstream Jazz Album' is verbatim from the wiki page; MB release date is a 2005 reissue but the release-group firstReleaseDate is 1996, so the 1996 year is consistent. All 5 keyTracks on the tracklist. The trailing biographical claim 'He achieved critical acclaim after winning two Grammy Awards for differing styles of jazz in 1998 and 2002' (apparently about Oscar Peterson) is not in the evidence and cannot be checked — unverifiable, not contradicted.

Embedded-MBID release confirms Kenny Garrett, 1996, Warner Bros. Records. All 5 keyTracks present in the tracklist. Personnel claims (quartet with guitarist Pat Metheny, bassist Rodney Whitaker, drummer Brian Blade) and 'seventh studio album' cannot be checked — MB ships no personnel — and are not contradicted. MB search match (sim 1.0) and stored wiki URL both about this Lateef/Ford album. albumDNA ('recorded 1994, released on the YAL label') is verbatim from Wikipedia. All 5 keyTracks on the tracklist. Embedded-MBID release confirms the album Touche, Kenny Wheeler (with Paul Bley), 1996-09-28, Justin Time Records. All 5 keyTracks present in the tracklist. The albumDNA is not about the album but is the opening biographical sentence of the Kenny Wheeler Wikipedia page (the stored wiki URL is the artist bio page, not an album page) — it is about the correct person and is factually consistent with that page (Canadian composer/trumpet & flugelhorn player, UK-based from the 1950s). Right entity throughout; wiki link is the artist's bio page rather than an album page, but it is the correct entity, not a disambiguation collision.

Embedded-MBID release confirms Beauty of Sunrise, Bheki Mseleku, 1997, Verve (FR). All 5 keyTracks present in the tracklist; the Bheki Mseleku Wikipedia discography even lists 'Beauty of Sunrise (PolyGram, 1997)'. albumDNA is the opening biographical sentence of the (correct) Mseleku wiki page — consistent with it. Wiki link is the artist bio page (correct person), not an album page.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: A 1997 Tudor date, Blue Rondo finds Charlie Parker in a bebop setting.
wrongClaims.problem: The embedded-MBID release is not a Charlie Parker bebop album; it is a 1997 Tudor various-artists clarinet recital. Parker (d. 1955) appears only as one composer credit among many (Debussy, Poulenc, Honegger, Stravinsky, Brubeck, etc.); the performers are clarinettist Karl-Heinz Steffens, pianist David Gazarov and others. Framing it as Parker 'in a bebop setting' belongs to a different kind of entity.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: artists: Brubeck, Debussy, Donato, Gilberto, Evans, Jones, Honegger, Parker, Poulenc, Stravinsky, Karl-Heinz Steffens, David Gazarov, Martin Zehn, Keith Copeland, Chris Lachotta; tracks include 'Premiere rhapsodie', 'Clarinet Sonatina, H.42', 'Clarinet Sonata, FP184'

notes: Items 'blue-rondo' (Charlie Parker) and 'blue-rondo-brubeck' (Dave Brubeck) both point to the SAME release mbid 505e34e7-b2da-4d89-92a9-72e2d9d1997d, a Tudor classical/jazz clarinet recital. The keyTracks listed here (Blue Rondo a la Turk, Premiere rhapsodie, Waltz for Debby, two Clarinet Sonatina movements) DO appear on that release, so keyTracks technically match; but the artist attribution to Charlie Parker and the 'bebop setting' description misrepresent a multi-composer clarinet album as a Parker bebop date. Classed entity_mismatch on the prose's factual skeleton (Parker/bebop) vs the actual release.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Pianist Dave Brubeck's 1997 album Blue Rondo draws from the cool jazz tradition, released on Tudor.
wrongClaims.problem: Same release mbid as the Charlie Parker 'blue-rondo' item: a 1997 Tudor various-artists clarinet recital, not a Dave Brubeck cool-jazz album. Brubeck appears only as one of many composer credits; the performers are clarinettist Karl-Heinz Steffens, pianist David Gazarov and others.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: artists: Brubeck, Debussy, Donato, Gilberto, Evans, Jones, Honegger, Parker, Poulenc, Stravinsky, Karl-Heinz Steffens, David Gazarov, Martin Zehn, Keith Copeland, Chris Lachotta

notes: Duplicate of the same Tudor compilation (mbid 505e34e7...) also tagged as the Charlie Parker 'blue-rondo' item. Here the framing is Dave Brubeck cool jazz, which the release contradicts. keyTracks also fail: only 'Blue Rondo a la Turk' is on the MB tracklist; 'How Does Your Garden Grow?', 'Festival Hall', 'Easy as You Go', 'Dizzy's Dream', 'I See Satie' are NOT on this release (they read like tracks from an actual Brubeck album, but per instructions I do not correct from memory — they simply do not match the evidence tracklist).

Embedded MBID and stored wiki URL both about Charles Lloyd's Canto. albumDNA is verbatim from the wiki extract (recorded Dec 1996, released ECM the following year; quartet of Bobo Stenson, Anders Jormin, Billy Hart) and the wiki Personnel section confirms that lineup. All 5 keyTracks on the tracklist.

Embedded-MBID release confirms title, 1997-09-09, Verve. Mark Whitfield is among the credited MB artists (Nicholas Payton, Mark Whitfield, Christian McBride), so crediting him is fine. The three tracks named in the prose and all 5 keyTracks are on the tracklist. Right entity, no contradictions.

MB search match (sim 1.0) confirms the album Forever Love, Mark Whitfield, 1997-03-04, Verve; all 5 keyTracks and the two songs named in prose (My One and Only Love, Nature Boy) are on the tracklist. The stored wiki URL is WRONG ENTITY: it points to 'Thomas Whitfield (singer)', a Detroit gospel singer (1954-1992), not Mark Whitfield the jazz guitarist — that page was discarded for fact-checking. The only substantive uncheckable claim is 'vocalist Diana Krall guests on a few tracks' (MB ships no personnel); not contradicted. Verdict unverifiable for the Krall claim; the site should fix the wrong Wikipedia link.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: firstReleaseDate: 1997 (release-group); wiki short-description: '1997 studio album by Dave Brubeck'

wrongClaims.ourClaim: In Their Own Sweet Way is a 1998 studio album by pianist Dave Brubeck and his quintet.
wrongClaims.problem: Prose dates the album to 1998, but the MB release-group firstReleaseDate and the wiki short-description both say 1997 (matching the item's own year field). The '1998' is inherited from an internal error in the Wikipedia extract body, which conflicts with its own short-description.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: shortDescription: "1997 studio album by Dave Brubeck"

notes: Right entity beyond doubt (MB sim 1.0; stored wiki URL correct; all 5 keyTracks on the tracklist; the 'four sons' detail is confirmed by the wiki Personnel list). Low-confidence factual_error: only the year-in-prose is wrong (says 1998; MB release-group and wiki short-description say 1997, as does the item's year field). The erroneous '1998' is copied verbatim from the Wikipedia extract body, which contradicts its own short-description — a Wikipedia typo reproduced in our prose. A repair-writer should change 'a 1998 studio album' to 1997.

MB MISMATCH: the embedded MBID 10623533-cb80-40fd-9206-2152fdee6418 resolves to a DIFFERENT album — 'Runnin' Wild' by the Boston Pops Orchestra, 1996, RCA Victor, a Glenn Miller-style pops program (A String of Pearls, Moonlight Serenade, In the Mood, etc.). It is not James P. Johnson's stride compilation, so it is discarded as evidence (not counter-evidence). None of our keyTracks (Runnin' Wild Medley, Carolina Shout, Arkansas Blues, Ole Miss Blues, Harlem Choc'late Babies On Parade) appear on that Boston Pops tracklist, so keyTracks cannot be validated (no_evidence). The stored wiki URL is the correct James P. Johnson artist-bio page, and the bio sentence in our prose (stride-piano pioneer; 'like Jelly Roll Morton, one of the key figures in the evolution of ragtime into what was eventually called jazz') is verbatim from it. So the right ARTIST is confirmed, but every album-specific claim (label Tradition Records, year 1997, the stride tracklist) is unverifiable because the MB release is the wrong album. The wrong MBID should be re-resolved.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Awarded Magister of Music at Fryderyk Chopin University of Music, Warsaw.
wrongClaims.problem: This biographical credential does not belong to Ben Webster (American tenor saxophonist, d. 1973) or to this album. It is lifted from the wrong-entity wiki page stored for this item — Tomasz Szukalski, a Polish saxophonist — where the identical sentence appears. A disambiguation-collision contaminant injected into the Ben Webster blurb.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Awarded Magister of Music (Master of Arts) at Fryderyk Chopin University of Music, Warsaw.

notes: Album core is clean: embedded-MBID release confirms Tenor Titans, Dexter Gordon & Ben Webster, 1997, Storyville Records; all 5 keyTracks on the tracklist; the two tracks named in prose match. BUT the stored wiki URL is WRONG ENTITY ('Tomasz Szukalski', a Polish saxophonist, d. 2012, not Ben Webster), and the final sentence of our albumDNA is a verbatim contaminant from that Szukalski page (his Warsaw conservatory degree) wrongly attached to the Ben Webster album. Flagged factual_error for that injected false credential and wrong_entity for the wiki link; a repair-writer must delete the Magister-of-Music sentence and fix the link.
